title: No Future
author: Lee Edelman
review: really struggled through this despite being quite entranced by its core message. i just cant get on board with some of these psychoanalyic just so stories. and i like esoteric bullshit! going to write something more substantial on this soon...

title: Tell Me I’m Worthless
author: Alison Rumfitt
review:
content warning (for this review and the book): transphobia, antisemitism, body horror, honestly a whole lot of stuff.

there is something fucking wrong with this country. this country is a rotting house, it's been here too long, it's a derelict house, it's a derelict collapsing damp house full of people living in derelict collapsing damp houses, houses which aren't homes, and which hate you, and which make you hate.

there's something wrong with this country, it's been here too long, it's bad stock, and everyone who lives here knows it, even if they can't diagnose it, and what they find to be wrong roots out everything worthwhile as well.

there is something wrong with this country, and there are worse things coming, but where else are you going to go, there's nowhere you can go where you would escape, any sanctuary of yours can be blown off the map, your future is here so you'd better not give it any more reasons to hate you.

there is something wrong with this country, and it lives inside you, and words don't help, analysis won't work, the call is coming from inside the house, and just like your wrong wrong wrong body, all you want is to pinpoint the root of the wrongness, and rip and tear it out.

Behind each sociable fun-loving eye
The private massacres are taking place --
The rich, all women, Jews, the human race.
- W.H. Auden, In Time of War

the worst / best choice to read as the November depression really starts to hit. made me cry at the end. made me tense as a coiled spring throughout. every time i read a trans book (like when I read Nevada) i devour it in one sitting, and disassociate for about a week afterwards. you could accuse some parts of it of being unsubtle, but that's ok - it's focused and clear eyed and furious. the split view shocked me, and got across what i've been trying to put a finger on about transphobia for a while, that the hurt is real, and the tragedy is that it's entirely divergent views of reality, insurmountable. the voice is incredible, the book moves from third to first person to direct address within a line, it feels like rage and grief spilling over, uncontainable. a lot of the time books which reference modern life (especially references to the internet) feel weirdly trite, but it works in this kind of horror, because it gets across how stomach churning it is to live right now. i'm glad it complicated the happy(ish) ending, but so glad it did end with some hope, just for my own mind.

For there is always a sanctuary more, a door that can never be forced, a last inviolable stronghold that can never be taken, whatever the attack; your vote can be taken, you name, you innards, or even your life, but that last stonghold can only be surrendered. And to surrender it for any reason other than love is to surrender love.'
- Ken Kesey

title: Minor Detail
author: Adania Shibli
review:
Christ.

Devastating, obviously. Structurally unpleasant to read - the past section is third person, and the present is first person, but both have this incredibly dissociative, traumatic quality.

Incredible imagery of the narrator driving around and around, not knowing what to do. The solider squashing bugs, over and over. So much repetition.

The narrators constant comparison of the old map of palestinian villages, and the new Israeli produced map, which is just all desert, the terra nullius zionists have fantasised about from the beginning. It really gets you to a place where you feel the dehumanising, brutal effect of this occupation, and how pointless it is, and how it degrades people's minds as well as their bodies, how it makes it impossible to tell a coherent story about yourself.

The anticlimax that we never do find out anything more about the girl, than what we heard entirely from the soldiers perspective. The skin crawling encounter with the museum worker, who speaks calmly and proudly about this little settlement.

The intense focus on the border in the back half reminded me of this section from The Emperor by Kapuściński -

To get things under control, to disarm the opposition, the authorities order a complete fetasha, covering everyone. We are searched incessantly. On the street, in the car, in front of the house, in the house, in the street, in front of the post office, in front of an office building, going into the editor's office, the movie theater, the church, in front of the bank, in front of the restaurant, in the marketplace, in the park. Anyone can search us, because we don't know who has the right and who hasn't, and asking only makes things worse. It's better to give in. Somebody's always searching us. Guys in rags with sticks, who don't say anything, but only stop us and hold out their arms, which is the signal for us to do the same: get ready to be searched. They take everything out of our briefcases and pockets, look at it, act surprised, screw up their faces, nod their heads, whisper advice to each other. They frisk us: bach, stomach, legs, shoes. And then what? Nothing, we can go on, until the next spreading of arms, until the next fetasha. The next one might be only a few steps on, and the whole thing starts all over again . The searchers never give you an acquittal, a general clearance, absolution. Every few minutes, every few steps, we have to clear ourselves again. [...] I had fetasha dreams. A multitude of dark, dirty, eager, creeping, dancing, searching hands covered me, squeezing, plucking, tickling, threatening to throttle me, until I awoke in sweat. I couldn't get back to sleep until morning.

Free palestine, death to the IDF.

title: Fictions
review: not sure what to make of this. i really liked it! but i also kinda struggled to get through it, and don't think i really understood what it was trying to do. and most of the stories didn't really stay with me afterwards? even tho i was kinda enjoying them in the moment. maybe i'll come back to it. you can see the influence on umberto eco pretty clearly. maybe i've just had too much of a run of heavy postmodern stuff, I want to get some nice trans lit in next. the story about the guy writing the book in a frozen moment before being executed was absolutely hellish.
author: Jorge Luis Borges

title: Giovanni’s Room
review:
just wonderful. difficult to say anything about it that hasn't been said before, better. it already had my heart, but then Hella returns from spain, and jesus fucking christ. That sense of the prison walls coming up, and the passion slowly fading, as you sit in your life and tell yourself 'this is everything i've wanted, this is everything i could want, isn't it?'. it felt genuinely suffocating to read. hella's character herself is wonderful, and their interactions are just so sad, superficially happy but affected and like playing a character, or a caricature. the way that hella is treated is itself so cruel, and yet there's no reason for it, it's just time and chance.

the way that time slows down in the book while he's with giovanni, how he was only actually living in the room for a few months, but it makes up the first 3/4ths, wheras months with hella are flattened into a few pages as you settle into a life that's not your own. the strange way that trans women are treated in the book, with venom, but venom that shows us as simply the bottom of a pecking order of self hatred, venom that comes from within (and which incidentally still acts as a 'we have always been here' bolster).

the way that emotion is tied so directly to place, that david can recall exactly how the room appeared, and his eventual break with giovanni is expressed subliminally(ish?) as a hatred for the filth of his room, and of paris as a whole.

this feeling that you have lived authentically, once, for such a brief period and it has shattered what you thought was your own happy life, and you can never ever return to it. make your own biblical readings.

the way that baldwin is writing before modern ideas of sexuality, or sidesteps existing ideas of strong binary sexuality - how both david and giovanni have had female partners, not just as 'beards', but as genuine parts of their lives, where david feels trapped by hella, giovanni longs to return to the innocence of life with 'his girl' (tho he says this in grief). to read this as a book 'about gay men' (or even 'bisexual' men) is entirely to miss the point.

the sense of being trapped in filth, in this filthy body, and the longing to escape it. his talk of his father, how david keeps him happy by telling him exactly the story he wants to hear, and loses respect for him by and by.

the incredible reading of both the repressor, and the bystander who (by chance) has fallen happily into the life path that's laid out for them and wrinkles their nose at queer lives:-

“You do not,” cried Giovanni, sitting up, “love anyone! You never have loved anyone, I am sure you never will! You love your purity, you love your mirror—you are just like a little virgin, you walk around with your hands in front of you as though you had some precious metal, gold, silver, rubies, maybe diamonds down there between your legs! You will never give it to anybody, you will never let anybody touch it—man or woman. You want to be clean. You think you came here covered with soap and you think you will go out covered with soap—and you do not want to stink, not even for five minutes, in the meantime.”

the ending, with david standing alone in the empty house, staring at himself in the mirror, and the capstone that punches you across time -

'The body in the mirror forces me to turn and face it. And I look at my body, which is under sentence of death. It is lean, hard, and cold, the incarnation of a mystery. And I do not know what moves in this body, what this body is searching. It is trapped in my mirror as it is trapped in time, and it hurries toward revelation.'

haven't we all felt like that.
author: James Baldwin

title: A Cyborg Manifesto
author: Donna J. Haraway
review: came at this after someone at a writing group reccomended it, and I liked it! i'd definitely benefit from rereading it, but even though it's only 88 pages it took me several hours to get through. it's refreshing to see that feminist theorists in the 80s recognised it as a uniquely revolutionary period, the way we do today. pointing out how common myths of original unity are, and how they imply the future revelation, apocalypse, and return to that original unity, is intriguing and unique. i've just started reading paul preciado's 'dysphoria mundi', and his idea of the 'somatheque' seems to owe a lot to this book (he does cite Haraway at the beginning). disheartening to see haraway dismiss 'innocence, and the collorary insistence on victimhood as the only ground for insight', when it feels like that logic has now expanded to be used by subaltern, oppressor, and genocidier alike. i think i'd need a load more context about where academic feminism was at in the 80's to really 'get' this manifesto.

title: The Empusium
review:
definitely drags in the middle, and although im sure thats intentional, it does still reduce the impact for me a bit. dr semperweiss said trans rights! his final speech is the core of this book for me, and its fantastic. the late revelation that wojnicz shares a squint with me was something else.

that final climax reminds me of gwynn in the etched city almost crossing over, and harry in disco elysium coming face to face with the insulindian phasmid. feels like this is a strange genre / story structure that id like to have a crack at myself.

god, next on my list HAS to be caliban and the witch. i fucking called the twist in it so hard that i spoiled it for myself. need a movie of this so i can do an empusium / conclave double bill. they can’t keep getting away with this!
author: Olga Tokarczuk

title: Can the Monster Speak?
author: Paul B. Preciado
review: my process of understanding myself ~as a trans woman~ (which is obv still in progress) involved simultaneously breaking down a lot of real self hatred for my apperance, and in coming to accept the way that trans women are seen and feared, to prepare myself to become one. i filtered a lot of that through the idea of the 'monster', and I'm deeply greatful to Precadio, Mieville and Cohen for helping me to do that.

title: Nevada
review:
yeah, i know. bite me. this basically resolved any lingering 'am i trans' thoughts. i've never felt so seen.

'Like, y'know, that feeling without words that I had for my whole life, like oh-my-god there is something seriously fucked up with my body and the way everybody is reading it.'
author: Imogen Binnie

title: The Etched City
review:
'While watching the slow execution, she had recalled an old story of a prophet who, at one point in his career, was met by an angel who cut him open from throat to groin. The angel then washed the prophet's heart with holy water, and filled it with gems symbolising knowledge and faith. She had thought, abstractly, 'Wouldn't we all like to imagine that we are so within, filled with valuable, beautiful and indestructible things, not this vulnerable and stinking offal?'

absolutely beautiful. a strange, dreamlike book, following a pretty evil gunslinger and a surgeon with a 'ghost conscience'. one of the best in the 'weird fiction' subgenre, which has been strangely forgotten - it's not even in print in the uk anymore?! cw for all sorts of horrible violence.

We modern demiurges are prolific copyists; we give few things souls of their own. Locomotives, with their close resemblance to beasts, may be the great exception; but in nearly all else with which todays poor humans are filling the world, I see a quelling of the numinous, an ashening of the fire of life. We are making an inert world; we are building a cemetery. And on the tombs, to remind us of life, we lay wreathes of poetry and bouquets of painting.

You expressed this very condition, when you said that art beautifies life. No longer integral, the numinous has become optional, a luxury - one of which you, my dear friend, are fond, however unconsciously. You adorn yourself with the same instincts as the primitive who puts a frightening mask of clay and feathers on his head, and you comport yourself in an uncommonly calculated way - as do I. We thus make numinous phenomena of ourselves. No mean trick - to make oneself a rarity, in this overpopulated age.

I have come to believe that we steer our individual spheres of being through the spectra of possible worlds via the choices we make, the acts we perform. Most people stick to known routes, and therefore cannot travel far. They live too modestly, and perhaps too privately. Only by being strange can we move, for strange acts cause us to be rejected by whatever normality we have offended, and to be propelled towards a normality that can better accommodate us.'
author: K.J. Bishop

title: The Emperor
review:
Indeed, my friend, from the moment smoke rose from the Palace a sort of negativism started to flood over us. I have trouble pinning it down, but you could feel negativism all around. You noticed it everywhere on people's faces, faces that seemed diminished and abandoned, without light or energy, in what people did and how they did it. There was negativism in what they said without speaking, in their absent being; as if shrunken, switched off; in their burnt-out existence, in their short range, small-stuff thinking; in their vegetable-patch, cottage-garden digging; in their weed-grown, overcast look; in the whole atmosphere; in all the immobility - despite the moving around - of the daily grind; in the climate; in the mincing steps. In everything you could feel the negativism flooding over us.

Even though the Emperor went on issuing decrees and striving to get things done, got up early and never rested, all the same the negativism was there, growing all the time, because from the day Germane committed suicide and his brother was captured to be hanged in the main square of the town, a negative system started operating between people and things. People seemed unable to control things; things existed and ceased to exist in their own malicious ways, slipping through peoples hands. Everyone felt helpless before the seemingly magic force by which things autonomously appeared and disappeared, and nobody knew how to master or break that force. This feeling of helplessness, of always losing, always falling behind the stronger, drove them deeper into negativism, into numbness, into dejection, into depression, into hiding like partridges.

Even conversation deteriorated, losing its vigour and momentum. Conversations started but somehow never seemed to be completed. They always reached an invisible but perceptible point, beyond which silence fell. The silence said, Everything is already known and clear, but clear in an obscure way, known unfathomably, dominating by being beyond helping. Having confirmed this truth by a moment of silence, the conversation changed its direction and moved on to a different subject, a trivial, second-rate, secondhand subject.
author: Ryszard Kapuściński

title: The Name of the Rose
author: Umberto Eco
review:
Really beautiful, I can't believe it took me this long to pick it up.

Others have said it but Eco's writing is so wonderful at properly putting you in the mind of people from history who genuinely thinks completely differently about the world. The argument that seeking knowledge and progress is a type of lust, and is actually incoherent, because everything worth knowing has already been written in scripture , is so alien to how we think about anything in the modern world, and it takes skill to make that hang together.

Along similar lines, this book showed me wonderfully just how millenarian and apocalyptic mediaeval christianity was, another completely alien viewpoint. Obviously modern Catholicism still holds these views, but for (some of) the monks in this book, 'the time is at hand' isn't just theoretical and far off, but literal and within their lifetimes. (Maybe this is still stressed in some Catholic teachings - I wasn't raised religious, so this is really far out of my experiences).

Jorge's motivations, to prevent laughter from legitimising the 'vulgar tongue', for me is tied together by a passage midway through, when the peasant girl is being captured as a witch:-

'For all her shouting, she was as if mute. There are words which give power, others that make us all the more derelict, and to this latter category belong the vulgar words of the simple, to whom the Lord has not granted the boon of self expression in the universal tongue of knowledge and power.'

So much there which you can still chew on today (not least what's happening right now in some other holy lands). Talking of that, the entire storyline with the peasant girl affected me much more than I was expecting. This passage almost brought me to tears, :-

'The only sure thing was that the girl would be burned. And I felt responsible, because it was as if she would also expiate on the pyre the sin I had committed with her. I burst shamefully into sobs and fled to my cell, where all through the night I chewed my pallet and moaned helplessly, for I was not even allowed – as they did in the romances of chivalry I had read with my companions at Melk - to lament and call out the beloveds name. This was the only earthly love of my life, and I could not, then or ever after, call that love by name.'

And that's it, we never see her again! Just an understated, beautiful, and tragic depiction of love, from a monk who can barely understand the concept, which takes an epistemic injustice (which the entire book is ofc about) and makes you feel the real consequences of it.

I so rarely reread books, but I know I'll come back to this one. Just wonderful.

title: Waiting for the Barbarians
review: now what's going to happen to us without barbarians? those people were a kind of solution...
author: J.M. Coetzee

title: One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
author: Ken Kesey
